output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: Flagged unsupported claims, overstated novelty, weak evidence, and methodological gaps; concrete revisions suggested below.

**Overstated novelty / priority claims.** Two "first" claims are made without citation or scoping:
- "first interface to embed clarifying questions directly into the search results page" — Bing/Google have shown inline "Did you mean / People also ask / refine your search" widgets for years, and the IR literature (e.g., Aliannejadi et al., Zamani et al. on clarifying questions in web search) has prototyped in-SERP clarification. Either cite and differentiate (e.g., "first to evaluate a mixed-initiative chip-based panel of model-generated clarifications in a controlled user study") or drop the claim.
- "first HCI study to measure user satisfaction with proactive disambiguation in open-domain search" — this is a strong claim that requires a literature audit. Trippas et al., Avula et al., and Kiesel et al. have measured user response to clarifications in conversational/spoken search. Restrict the scope or remove.

**Evidence does not support the conclusions.**
- "22% fewer query reformulations" is reported only for "participants who engaged with the panel" vs. the control group. This is a self-selection comparison, not a causal effect of the interface. The correct comparison is intent-to-treat: all participants assigned to the ClarifyLens condition vs. all controls. Report both, and note the selection bias.
- No statistical tests, confidence intervals, sample sizes per condition, or effect sizes are given. With n=48 across six tasks, a 0.6-point Likert difference and a 22% reduction need significance testing (and ideally mixed-effects modeling with task and participant as random effects).
- The qualitative quotes ("helpful when I didn't know…", "less annoying than I expected") are cherry-picked. State how quotes were selected, how many participants made similar comments, and report any negative themes.
- "reached a satisfactory result" is undefined. Who judged satisfaction — the participant, an annotator, a gold label? This must be operationalized.

**Unsupported leaps in the contribution framing.**
- "establishes a new design pattern" and "foundational step toward truly mixed-initiative search interfaces" are not warranted by a single 48-person lab study on six tasks. Soften to "suggests" / "provides initial evidence."
- "We expect this design pattern to generalize to voice assistants, enterprise search, and customer-support chat" — these modalities differ substantially (no visual chips in voice; enterprise queries have different ambiguity distributions). Either remove or explicitly mark as speculation and motivate.
- "practitioners can adopt the pattern without retraining their retrieval stack" — true only if the question-generation model is already available; the cost of that model is ignored.

**Methodological gaps that should be disclosed in the section.**
- Study design (between- vs. within-subjects), assignment, counterbalancing of tasks, and what the control condition actually was.
- How the three candidate questions are selected and ranked (even if the model is a black box, the selection/ranking layer is part of the interface contribution).
- Whether the "facet diversity score" calibration was validated, and the ambiguity distribution across the six tasks.
- Demographics and the limits of a university participant pool for generalizing to open-domain search users.

**Internal tension to resolve.** The section claims the contribution is "squarely an interface contribution rather than a modeling one," but the headline outcomes depend on the quality of the (black-box) generated questions. If the model produced poor questions, the panel would presumably hurt. Acknowledge this confound or report a manipulation check (e.g., human-rated question quality).

**Suggested rewrite of the closing paragraph (tightened, claims scoped):**

"In a 48-participant study, participants who engaged with the clarifying-question panel reached a self-rated satisfactory result with fewer reformulations and reported modestly higher task confidence than controls; the effect under intent-to-treat analysis is smaller but directionally consistent (report numbers). These results provide initial evidence that an inline, chip-based clarification panel is acceptable to users and may reduce reformulation effort in ambiguous open-domain queries. Because the question-generation model is treated as a black box, our contribution is the interface and its evaluation, not the underlying NLG; the magnitude of the effect is therefore bounded by question quality, which we did not manipulate. Generalization to voice and enterprise settings — where the visual affordance of chips is unavailable or the ambiguity distribution differs — remains open."

**Smaller prose issues.**
- "judged ambiguous" — by what? Specify the trigger (model confidence, retrieval entropy, etc.) early.
- "standard hourly rate" — give the figure for reproducibility.
- "calibrated using the TREC-style facet diversity score" — cite the specific definition; "TREC-style" is vague.
- Twice-used "we believe" weakens the claims; either show evidence or omit.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the specific prior-art citations (Aliannejadi, Zamani, Trippas, Avula, Kiesel) against the section's exact scope — these are the most likely overlapping works based on the subfield, but the authors should confirm against their own lit review before adopting the rescoped "first" claims.
